points.name: A Yin
points.location:
Basic A Point Yin aspect is located approximately 1 cm lateral to the midline on both sides of the head, at the frontal hairline, and about 5 cm anterior to the coronal suture. 
Point A1 lies approximately 1 cm below the frontal hairline.
points.shortDescription: Points A1–A8 correspond to the cervical spine and the regions of the body innervated by the cervical nerves.
points.longDescription: Basic Point A is divided into eight equal segments, designated A1–A8, along a 2 cm line running from anterior to posterior. Segment A1 is located about 1 cm below the hairline, A3 lies directly on the hairline, and A8 is positioned approximately 1 cm above the hairline.
points.reactionArea: The area between the head and cervical spine from C1 to C7.
indications: Point A generally used for all reversible conditions in areas innervated by the cervical nerves, both superficial and deep.
Pain relief following injury or surgery
Headaches and migraines of any origin
tags: Pain relief following injury or surgery
Headaches and migraines of any origin

points.name: A Yin
points.location:
Basic A Point Yin aspect is located approximately 1 cm lateral to the midline on both sides of the head, at the frontal hairline, and about 5 cm anterior to the coronal suture. 
Point A8 Yin lies approximately 1 cm above the frontal hairline.
points.shortDescription: Points A1–A8 correspond to the cervical spine and the regions of the body innervated by the cervical nerves.
points.longDescription: Basic Point A Yin is divided into eight equal segments, designated A1–A8, along a 2 cm line running from anterior to posterior. Segment A1 is located about 1 cm below the hairline, A3 lies directly on the hairline, and A8 is positioned approximately 1 cm above the hairline.
points.reactionArea: The area between the head and cervical spine from C1 to C7.
indications: Point A generally used for all reversible conditions in areas innervated by the cervical nerves, both superficial and deep.
Pain relief following injury or surgery
Headaches and migraines of any origin
tags: Pain relief following injury or surgery
Headaches and migraines of any origin

points.name: A Yang
points.location: The point is situated on a 1.5 cm vertical line extending from 1cm above the lambdoid suture to 1 cm below it. This line lies approximately 1 cm lateral to the mid-sagittal line, corresponding to a position 1 cm lateral to the DU-19 acupuncture point, located at the junction of the sagittal and lambdoid sutures.
points.shortDescription: Points A1–A8 correspond to the head and cervical spine and the regions of the body innervated by the cervical nerves.
points.longDescription: Basic Point A Yang is divided into eight equal segments, designated A1–A8, along a 2 cm line running from anterior to posterior.
points.reactionArea: The area between the head and cervical spine from C1 to C7.
indications: Point A generally used for all reversible conditions in areas innervated by the cervical nerves, both superficial and deep.
Pain relief following injury or surgery
Headaches and migraines of any origin
tags: Pain relief following injury or surgery
Headaches and migraines of any origin

points.name: A Yang
points.location: The point is situated on a 1.5 cm vertical line extending from 1cm above the lambdoid suture to 1 cm below it. This line lies approximately 1 cm lateral to the mid-sagittal line, corresponding to a position 1 cm lateral to the DU-19 acupuncture point, located at the junction of the sagittal and lambdoid sutures.
points.shortDescription: Points A1–A8 correspond to the head and cervical spine and the regions of the body innervated by the cervical nerves.
points.longDescription: Basic Point A Yang is divided into eight equal segments, designated A1–A8, along a 2 cm line running from anterior to posterior.
points.reactionArea: The area between the head and cervical spine from C1 to C7.
indications: Point A generally used for all reversible conditions in areas innervated by the cervical nerves, both superficial and deep.
Pain relief following injury or surgery
Headaches and migraines of any origin

points.name: B Yin
points.location: Basic Points B Yin  aspect are approx. 1 cm bilateral to the point A, or 2 cm lateral to the midline on the hairline. As the hairline might be dificult to find, use the skinfold of the wrinkled forehead as a guide. The point B is 1 cm above this line.
points.shortDescription: Basic Point B correspond to the cervical spine and the regions of the body innervated by the cervical nerves.
points.longDescription: Basic Point B has no known subdivisions.
points.reactionArea: The area between the neck and shoulder.
indications: Reversible conditions affecting areas innervated by the cervical nerves, including both internal and external structures, as well as superficial and deep tissues
Shoulder pain resulting from immobility following an arm
Conditions affecting the region between the supraclavicular fossa and the supraspinous fossa
Shoulder pain accompanied by arm weakness or
Trapezius muscle spasm, particularly when associated with headaches

points.name: B Yang
points.location: Basic Point B (Yang) is situated in the occipital area, about 0.7 cm lateral to Basic Point A (Yang). It is located on the lambdoid suture, approximately 1.5 cm from the mid-sagittal line.
points.shortDescription: Point B correspond to the cervical spine and the regions of the body innervated by the cervical nerves.
points.longDescription: Basic pont B has no know subdivisions.
points.reactionArea: The area between the neck and shoulder.
indications: Reversible conditions affecting areas innervated by the cervical nerves, including both internal and external structures, as well as superficial and deep tissues
Shoulder pain resulting from immobility following an arm
Conditions affecting the region between the supraclavicular fossa and the supraspinous fossa
Shoulder pain accompanied by arm weakness or
Trapezius muscle spasm, particularly when associated with headaches

points.name: C Yin
points.shortDescription: Basic Point C Yin represents the upper extremity and can be divided into nine segments corresponding to the shoulder, arm, forearm, hand, and fingers.
points.longDescription: Basic Point C Yin is a major YNSA scalp acupuncture point associated with the upper limb. It represents the entire upper extremity and may be subdivided into nine smaller segments for more precise treatment. From superior to inferior, these segments correspond to the shoulder joint, upper arm, elbow, forearm, hand, and fingers. The point follows an imaginary line running at approximately 45 degrees from the root of the nose toward the junction of the frontalis and temporalis muscles. The hand region is located approximately 1 cm below the hairline, with the thumb positioned medially.
points.location: Located approximately 2.5 cm lateral to Basic Point B and 4.5–5 cm from the midline. The point extends for about 2 cm in a slightly oblique line that follows the contour of the skull. It runs along an imaginary 45-degree line from the root of the nose to the border between the frontalis and temporalis muscles.
points.reactionArea: The area between the shoulder and the palm of the hand.

points.name: C Yang
points.shortDescription: Basic Point C Yang represents the upper extremity on the occipital aspect of the scalp, sharing the same anatomical arrangement and indications as C Yin.
points.longDescription: Basic Point C Yang follows the same anatomical arrangement as the Yin point and lies approximately 1 cm inferior to it, over the lambdoid suture. It shares the same functional indications as C Yin and is used for upper extremity disorders including shoulder, arm, forearm, hand, and finger conditions.
points.location: This point lies along an oblique line approximately 1.5 cm in length, extending from a position 0.75 cm above the lambdoid suture to 0.75 cm below it. The line is situated about 3.5 cm lateral to the mid-sagittal line. To identify the point, draw a line at a 45° angle from the external occipital protuberance toward the lambdoid suture; the point is located at the intersection of this line with the lambdoid suture.
points.reactionArea: The area between the shoulder and the palm of the hand.

points.name: D Yin
points.shortDescription: Basic Point D Yin represents the lumbar spine, sacrum, coccyx, and lower extremities, with subdivisions allowing precise treatment of individual lumbar segments.
points.longDescription: Basic Point D Yin is a major YNSA scalp acupuncture point associated with the lower body, including the lumbar spine, sacrum, coccyx, and lower extremities. The point is further divided into six smaller segments, D1–D6, which correspond to specific anatomical regions of the lower spine. D1 corresponds to the L1 vertebra, D2–D5 correspond to L2–L5, and D6 represents the sacrum and coccyx. These points form a vertical line resembling a string of beads and allow highly targeted treatment of individual lumbar vertebral levels, including pain, nerve irritation, and functional disturbances.
points.location: Located in the temporal region on the hairline, approximately 1 cm above the zygomatic arch and 2 cm anterior to the ear, over the temporalis muscle. The D1–D6 subdivisions are situated immediately anterior to the ear above the temporomandibular joint, forming a vertical line approximately 1 cm in length.
points.reactionArea: The area of the lumbar spine from the diaphragm to the feet.
indications: Disorders of internal organs related to lumbar nerve distribution

points.name: D Yang
points.shortDescription: Basic Point D Yang represents the lower body and is located over the sphenoid bone toward the border of the temporal bone.
points.longDescription: Basic Point D Yang follows the same general representation of the lower body as D Yin but does not reflect the D1–D6 subdivisions as precisely. The Yang divisions are positioned behind the pinna in a slightly curved horizontal line measuring approximately 1–1.5 cm in length.
points.location: Located over the sphenoid bone toward the border of the temporal bone. The corresponding subdivisions are positioned behind the pinna in a slightly curved horizontal line approximately 1–1.5 cm in length.
points.reactionArea: The area of the lumbar spine from the diaphragm to the feet.
indications: Disorders of internal organs related to lumbar nerve distribution

points.name: D1 Yin
points.shortDescription: D1 Yin targets the L1 lumbar vertebra and its associated structures.
points.longDescription: Basic Point D Yin is a major YNSA scalp acupuncture point associated with the lower body, including the lumbar spine, sacrum, coccyx, and lower extremities. The point is further divided into six smaller segments, D1–D6, which correspond to specific anatomical regions of the lower spine. D1 corresponds to the L1 vertebra, D2–D5 correspond to L2–L5, and D6 represents the sacrum and coccyx. These points form a vertical line resembling a string of beads and allow highly targeted treatment of individual lumbar vertebral levels, including pain, nerve irritation, and functional disturbances.
points.location: Part of the D1–D6 Yin subdivision, situated immediately anterior to the ear above the temporomandibular joint. D1 is the most superior point in the vertical line.
points.reactionArea: Lumbar vertebra L1.
indications: Disorders of internal organs related to lumbar nerve distribution

points.name: D2 Yin
points.shortDescription: D2 Yin targets the L2 lumbar vertebra and its associated structures.
points.longDescription: Basic Point D Yin is a major YNSA scalp acupuncture point associated with the lower body, including the lumbar spine, sacrum, coccyx, and lower extremities. The point is further divided into six smaller segments, D1–D6, which correspond to specific anatomical regions of the lower spine. D1 corresponds to the L1 vertebra, D2–D5 correspond to L2–L5, and D6 represents the sacrum and coccyx. These points form a vertical line resembling a string of beads and allow highly targeted treatment of individual lumbar vertebral levels, including pain, nerve irritation, and functional disturbances.
points.location: Part of the D1–D6 Yin subdivision, situated immediately anterior to the ear above the temporomandibular joint, second from the top of the vertical line.
points.reactionArea: Lumbar vertebra L2.
indications: Disorders of internal organs related to lumbar nerve distribution

points.name: D3 Yin
points.shortDescription: D3 Yin targets the L3 lumbar vertebra and its associated structures.
points.longDescription: Basic Point D Yin is a major YNSA scalp acupuncture point associated with the lower body, including the lumbar spine, sacrum, coccyx, and lower extremities. The point is further divided into six smaller segments, D1–D6, which correspond to specific anatomical regions of the lower spine. D1 corresponds to the L1 vertebra, D2–D5 correspond to L2–L5, and D6 represents the sacrum and coccyx. These points form a vertical line resembling a string of beads and allow highly targeted treatment of individual lumbar vertebral levels, including pain, nerve irritation, and functional disturbances.
points.location: Part of the D1–D6 Yin subdivision, situated immediately anterior to the ear above the temporomandibular joint, middle of the vertical line.
points.reactionArea: Lumbar vertebra L3.
indications: Disorders of internal organs related to lumbar nerve distribution

points.name: D4 Yin
points.shortDescription: D4 Yin targets the L4 lumbar vertebra and its associated structures.
points.longDescription: Basic Point D Yin is a major YNSA scalp acupuncture point associated with the lower body, including the lumbar spine, sacrum, coccyx, and lower extremities. The point is further divided into six smaller segments, D1–D6, which correspond to specific anatomical regions of the lower spine. points.location: Part of the D1–D6 Yin subdivision, situated immediately anterior to the ear above the temporomandibular joint, second from the bottom of the vertical line.
points.reactionArea: Lumbar vertebra L4.
indications: Disorders of internal organs related to lumbar nerve distribution

points.name: D5 Yin
points.shortDescription: D5 Yin targets the L5 lumbar vertebra and its associated structures.
points.longDescription: Basic Point D Yin is a major YNSA scalp acupuncture point associated with the lower body, including the lumbar spine, sacrum, coccyx, and lower extremities. The point is further divided into six smaller segments, D1–D6, which correspond to specific anatomical regions of the lower spine. D1 corresponds to the L1 vertebra, D2–D5 correspond to L2–L5, and D6 represents the sacrum and coccyx. These points form a vertical line resembling a string of beads and allow highly targeted treatment of individual lumbar vertebral levels, including pain, nerve irritation, and functional disturbances.
points.location: Part of the D1–D6 Yin subdivision, situated immediately anterior to the ear above the temporomandibular joint, second from the bottom of the vertical line.
points.reactionArea: Lumbar vertebra L5.
indications: Disorders of internal organs related to lumbar nerve distribution

points.name: D6 Yin
points.shortDescription: D6 Yin targets the sacrum and coccyx.
points.longDescription: Basic Point D Yin is a major YNSA scalp acupuncture point associated with the lower body, including the lumbar spine, sacrum, coccyx, and lower extremities. The point is further divided into six smaller segments, D1–D6, which correspond to specific anatomical regions of the lower spine. D1 corresponds to the L1 vertebra, D2–D5 correspond to L2–L5, and D6 represents the sacrum and coccyx. These points form a vertical line resembling a string of beads and allow highly targeted treatment of individual lumbar vertebral levels, including pain, nerve irritation, and functional disturbances.
points.location: Part of the D1–D6 Yin subdivision, situated immediately anterior to the ear above the temporomandibular joint. D6 is the most inferior point in the vertical line.
points.reactionArea: Coccyx.
indications: Disorders of internal organs related to lumbar nerve distribution

points.name: D1 Yang
points.shortDescription: D1 Yang targets the L1 lumbar vertebra and its associated structures.
points.longDescription: Basic Point D Yang follows the same general representation of the lower body as D Yin but does not reflect the D1–D6 subdivisions as precisely. The Yang divisions are positioned behind the pinna in a slightly curved horizontal line measuring approximately 1–1.5 cm in length.
points.location: Part of the D1–D6 Yang subdivision, positioned behind the pinna in a slightly curved horizontal line. D1 Yang is the most anterior point of the line.
points.reactionArea: Lumbar vertebra L1.
indications: Disorders of internal organs related to lumbar nerve distribution

points.name: D2 Yang
points.shortDescription: D2 Yang targets the L2 lumbar vertebra and its associated structures.
points.longDescription: Basic Point D Yang follows the same general representation of the lower body as D Yin but does not reflect the D1–D6 subdivisions as precisely. The Yang divisions are positioned behind the pinna in a slightly curved horizontal line measuring approximately 1–1.5 cm in length.
points.location: Part of the D1–D6 Yang subdivision, positioned behind the pinna in a slightly curved horizontal line, second from the anterior end.
points.reactionArea: Lumbar vertebra L2.
indications: Disorders of internal organs related to lumbar nerve distribution

points.name: D3 Yang
points.shortDescription: D3 Yang targets the L3 lumbar vertebra and its associated structures.
points.longDescription: Basic Point D Yang follows the same general representation of the lower body as D Yin but does not reflect the D1–D6 subdivisions as precisely. The Yang divisions are positioned behind the pinna in a slightly curved horizontal line measuring approximately 1–1.5 cm in length.
points.location: Part of the D1–D6 Yang subdivision, positioned behind the pinna in a slightly curved horizontal line, middle of the line.
points.reactionArea: Lumbar vertebra L3.
indications: Disorders of internal organs related to lumbar nerve distribution

points.name: D4 Yang
points.shortDescription: D4 Yang targets the L4 lumbar vertebra and its associated structures.
points.longDescription: Basic Point D Yang follows the same general representation of the lower body as D Yin but does not reflect the D1–D6 subdivisions as precisely. The Yang divisions are positioned behind the pinna in a slightly curved horizontal line measuring approximately 1–1.5 cm in length.
points.location: Part of the D1–D6 Yang subdivision, positioned behind the pinna in a slightly curved horizontal line, second from the posterior end.
points.reactionArea: Lumbar vertebra L4.
indications: Disorders of internal organs related to lumbar nerve distribution

points.name: D5 Yang
points.shortDescription: D5 Yang targets the L5 lumbar vertebra and its associated structures.
points.longDescription: Basic Point D Yang follows the same general representation of the lower body as D Yin but does not reflect the D1–D6 subdivisions as precisely. The Yang divisions are positioned behind the pinna in a slightly curved horizontal line measuring approximately 1–1.5 cm in length.
points.location: Part of the D1–D6 Yang subdivision, positioned behind the pinna in a slightly curved horizontal line, second from the posterior end.
points.reactionArea: Lumbar vertebra L5.
indications: Disorders of internal organs related to lumbar nerve distribution

points.name: D6 Yang
points.shortDescription: D6 Yang targets the sacrum and coccyx.
points.longDescription: Basic Point D Yang follows the same general representation of the lower body as D Yin but does not reflect the D1–D6 subdivisions as precisely. The Yang divisions are positioned behind the pinna in a slightly curved horizontal line measuring approximately 1–1.5 cm in length.
points.location: Part of the D1–D6 Yang subdivision, positioned behind the pinna in a slightly curved horizontal line. D6 Yang is the most posterior point of the line.
points.reactionArea: Coccyx.
indications: Disorders of internal organs related to lumbar nerve distribution

points.name: E Yin
points.shortDescription: Basic Point E Yin represents the thoracic spine, ribs, thoracic cavity, and internal organs associated with thoracic nerve distribution.
points.longDescription: Basic Point E Yin is a YNSA scalp acupuncture point associated with the thoracic vertebrae, rib cage, thoracic cavity, and the internal organs innervated by the thoracic nerves. The point is divided into twelve segments, E1–E12, corresponding to the twelve thoracic vertebrae. E1, located at the superior end of the line, represents the T1 vertebra, while E12, located closest to the nose, corresponds to T12. This segmental arrangement allows for targeted treatment of specific thoracic spinal levels and their associated structures.
points.location: Located above the eyebrow, beginning approximately 1 cm lateral to the midline. The point follows a laterally directed oblique line at an angle of approximately 15 degrees, approximately 2 cm in length, present bilaterally. The twelve subdivisions E1–E12 are arranged sequentially along this line; E1 is located at the superior end while E12 is nearest to the nose.
points.reactionArea: The area of the thoracic spine from T1 to T12.
indications: Internal organ disorders related to thoracic nerve distribution

points.name: E Yang
points.shortDescription: Basic Point E Yang represents the thoracic vertebrae and associated structures, located in the occipital Yang region.
points.longDescription: Basic Point E Yang is located in the corresponding occipital Yang position and shares the same functional indications as E Yin. It is used for thoracic disorders, cardiopulmonary conditions, and internal organ disorders related to thoracic nerve distribution.
points.location: The point lies on a crest above the superior nuchal line along a 2 cm horizontal line extending from approximately 3 cm lateral to the mid-sagittal line to about 1 cm lateral to the mid-sagittal line.
points.reactionArea: The area of the thoracic spine from T1 to T12.
indications: Internal organ disorders related to thoracic nerve distribution

points.name: F
points.shortDescription: Basic Point F represents the sciatic nerve and is used in combination with Basic Point D points.
points.longDescription: Basic Point F is a bilateral YNSA scalp acupuncture point with only a Yang representation. It is primarily associated with the sciatic nerve and is used as a complementary point in combination with Basic Point D Yin or D Yang. It is not typically used as a standalone treatment point but enhances the therapeutic effect when combined with lumbar and lower limb representations.
points.location: Located behind the ear at the highest point of the mastoid process on both sides.
points.reactionArea: The area of the buttocks (gluteal muscles).

points.name: G Yin
points.shortDescription: Basic Point G Yin represents the knee joint and patella with subdivisions for medial, frontal, and lateral aspects.
points.longDescription: Basic Point G Yin is divided into three subpoints representing different regions of the knee joint. G1 corresponds to the medial knee, G2 to the frontal knee, and G3 to the lateral knee. G1 is the most anterior. These points are located above Basic Point D and are used for precise treatment of knee-related disorders. The segmentation allows targeted therapy for specific anatomical structures of the knee joint.
points.location: Located above Basic Point D. G1 is the most anterior point, followed by G2 and G3 arranged laterally.
points.reactionArea: The area of the knee.

points.name: G Yang
points.shortDescription: Basic Point G Yang mirrors knee function and is used for knee disorders and pain conditions.
points.longDescription: Basic Point G Yang corresponds to the knee joint in the Yang system and reflects the same functional representation as G Yin. It is used for the treatment of knee-related disorders including pain, trauma, and degenerative conditions. The point is stimulated around the edge of the mastoid process and follows the same therapeutic logic as its Yin counterpart.
points.location: Located around the edge of the mastoid process on the posterior aspect of the scalp.
points.reactionArea: The area of the knee.

points.name: G1 Yin
points.shortDescription: G1 Yin represents the medial aspect of the knee joint.
points.longDescription: Basic Point G Yin is divided into three subpoints representing different regions of the knee joint. G1 corresponds to the medial knee, G2 to the frontal knee, and G3 to the lateral knee. G1 is the most anterior. These points are located above Basic Point D and are used for precise treatment of knee-related disorders.
points.location: Located above Basic Point D, the most anterior of the three G Yin subpoints.
points.reactionArea: Medial knee.

points.name: G2 Yin
points.shortDescription: G2 Yin represents the frontal aspect of the knee joint.
points.longDescription: Basic Point G Yin is divided into three subpoints representing different regions of the knee joint. G1 corresponds to the medial knee, G2 to the frontal knee, and G3 to the lateral knee. These points are located above Basic Point D and are used for precise treatment of knee-related disorders.
points.location: Located above Basic Point D, between G1 and G3.
points.reactionArea: Frontal knee.

points.name: G3 Yin
points.shortDescription: G3 Yin represents the lateral aspect of the knee joint.
points.longDescription: Basic Point G Yin is divided into three subpoints representing different regions of the knee joint. G1 corresponds to the medial knee, G2 to the frontal knee, and G3 to the lateral knee. G3 is the most lateral. These points are located above Basic Point D and are used for precise treatment of knee-related disorders.
points.location: Located above Basic Point D, the most lateral of the three G Yin subpoints.
points.reactionArea: Lateral knee.

points.name: G1 Yang
points.shortDescription: G1 Yang represents the medial aspect of the knee joint in the occipital Yang region.
points.longDescription: Basic Point G Yang corresponds to the knee joint in the Yang system. G1 Yang is the most superior of the three G Yang subpoints and is used for treatment of knee-related disorders including pain, trauma, and degenerative conditions.
points.location: Located around the edge of the mastoid process, the most superior of the three G Yang subpoints.
points.reactionArea: Medial knee.

points.name: G2 Yang
points.shortDescription: G2 Yang represents the frontal aspect of the knee joint in the occipital Yang region.
points.longDescription: Basic Point G Yang corresponds to the knee joint in the Yang system. G2 Yang is the middle of the three G Yang subpoints and is used for treatment of knee-related disorders including pain, trauma, and degenerative conditions.
points.location: Located around the edge of the mastoid process, between G1 Yang and G3 Yang.
points.reactionArea: Frontal knee.

points.name: G3 Yang
points.shortDescription: G3 Yang represents the lateral aspect of the knee joint in the occipital Yang region.
points.longDescription: Basic Point G Yang corresponds to the knee joint in the Yang system. G3 Yang is the most inferior of the three G Yang subpoints and is used for treatment of knee-related disorders including pain, trauma, and degenerative conditions.
points.location: Located around the edge of the mastoid process, the most inferior of the three G Yang subpoints.
points.reactionArea: Lateral knee.

points.name: H
points.shortDescription: Basic Point H is a complementary lumbar point used to enhance the effect of Basic Point D.
points.longDescription: Basic Point H is an auxiliary YNSA point used in combination with Basic Point D. It is rarely used alone and serves to enhance treatment outcomes in chronic lumbar and lower limb disorders. The point becomes more prominent and palpable in cases of lumbago or lower extremity dysfunction, reflecting its diagnostic and therapeutic relevance.
points.location: Located 1 cm above Basic Point B.
points.reactionArea: The lower extremities, and the knee.

points.name: I
points.shortDescription: Basic Point I is a complementary lumbar point used alongside Basic Point D for chronic pain conditions.
points.longDescription: Basic Point I is a supplementary YNSA point used in combination with Basic Point D. It is rarely used as a standalone point and is primarily indicated for chronic lumbar pain and lower limb dysfunction. Like Basic Point H, it becomes more palpable in pathological conditions and enhances the therapeutic effect of D points.
points.location: Identify the apex of the ear and draw an imaginary apex line extending vertically upward from it. Yin aspect: Located approximately 4 cm above the ear apex and 2–3 cm in front of the apex line. Yang aspect: Located approximately 4 cm above the ear apex and 2–3 cm behind the apex line.
points.reactionArea: Lower extremities. Located in the temple region above the ear, the I point has become a key point for pain treatment and has developed into its own somatotope. This somatotope is believed to represent the whole body, and needling the point may have therapeutic effects on the body as a whole.

points.name: Ear Point
points.shortDescription: The Ear sensory point treats auricular disorders including otitis, tinnitus, and ear pain.
points.longDescription: The YNSA Sensory Points (Eye, Nose, Mouth, Ear) are arranged vertically in the frontal yin zone at approximately 1 cm intervals, starting from the lowest Basic A point and situated 1 cm lateral to the midline. A corresponding Yang representation of all sensory points is located in the occipital region. The Ear point is positioned approximately 1.5 cm from Basic C on a 45-degree line drawn between Basic C and the root of the nose.
points.location: Approximately 1.5 cm from Basic Point C, on a 45-degree line drawn between Basic C and the root of the nose.
points.reactionArea: The ear and surrounding auricular structures.

points.name: Extra Ear Point Yin
points.shortDescription: Extra Ear Points are complementary points to treat tinnitus. For tinnitus treatment needle both Ear Yin and Yang points in combination with both Extra Ear Yin and Yang.
points.longDescription: Extra Ear Point Yin is a complementary sensory point used alongside the Ear Yin and Ear Yang points specifically for tinnitus treatment. It is not used in isolation; optimal results are achieved when all four ear points (Ear Yin, Ear Yang, Extra Ear Yin, Extra Ear Yang) are combined.
points.location: Midway between Ear Yin and Ear Yang, approximately 3 cm above the line connecting the Ear Yin and Ear Yang points.
points.reactionArea: The ear and surrounding auricular structures.

points.name: Extra Ear Point Yang
points.shortDescription: Extra Ear Points are complementary points to treat tinnitus. For tinnitus treatment needle both Ear Yin and Yang points in combination with both Extra Ear Yin and Yang.
points.longDescription: Extra Ear Point Yang is a complementary sensory point used alongside the Ear Yin and Ear Yang points specifically for tinnitus treatment. It is not used in isolation; optimal results are achieved when all four ear points (Ear Yin, Ear Yang, Extra Ear Yin, Extra Ear Yang) are combined.
points.location: Midway between Ear Yin and Ear Yang, approximately 3 cm above the line connecting the Ear Yin and Ear Yang points.
points.reactionArea: The ear and surrounding auricular structures.

points.name: Eye Point
points.shortDescription: The Eye sensory point treats ophthalmic disorders and ocular pain.
points.longDescription: The YNSA Sensory Points (Eye, Nose, Mouth, Ear) are arranged vertically in the frontal yin zone at approximately 1 cm intervals, starting from the lowest Basic A point and situated 1 cm lateral to the midline. A corresponding Yang representation of all sensory points is located in the occipital region.
points.location: Arranged vertically with the other sensory points (Nose, Mouth) at approximately 1 cm intervals, starting from the lowest Basic A point, situated 1 cm lateral to the midline.
points.reactionArea: The eye and visual system.

points.name: Nose Point
points.shortDescription: The Nose sensory point treats conditions affecting the nose and nasal passages.
points.longDescription: The YNSA Sensory Points (Eye, Nose, Mouth, Ear) are arranged vertically in the frontal yin zone at approximately 1 cm intervals, starting from the lowest Basic A point and situated 1 cm lateral to the midline. A corresponding Yang representation of all sensory points is located in the occipital region.
points.location: Arranged vertically with the other sensory points (Eye, Mouth) at approximately 1 cm intervals, starting from the lowest Basic A point, situated 1 cm lateral to the midline.
points.reactionArea: The nose and nasal passages.

points.name: Cerebrum Point Yin
points.shortDescription: The Cerebrum Point is one of three YNSA Brain Points used to treat neurological and neuropsychiatric conditions. It has corresponding diagnostic zones on the abdomen and neck.
points.longDescription: The three YNSA Brain Points — Cerebrum, Cerebellum, and Basal Ganglia — form a distinct point group for treating neurological diseases. In the Yin (frontal) aspect, the Cerebrum Point lies approximately 1 cm lateral to the midline on both sides, along a line extending posteriorly from Basic Point A8. The group begins approximately 3 cm anterior to the coronal suture and extends 1.5–2 cm toward Basic Point A8, starting just posterior to the edge of the galea aponeurotica.
points.location: Approximately 1 cm lateral to the midline on both sides of the head, along a posteriorly directed line from Basic Point A8. The Brain Points originate just posterior to the galea aponeurotica, beginning about 3 cm anterior to the coronal suture and extending approximately 1.5–2 cm toward Basic Point A8.
points.reactionArea: The cerebrum and its associated cortical functions.

points.name: Cerebrum Point Yang
points.shortDescription: The Cerebrum Point Yang is the occipital counterpart to the Yin Brain Point, sharing the same neurological indications.
points.longDescription: The Yang (occipital) Brain Points mirror the Yin group in anatomical arrangement and clinical application. The Cerebrum Point Yang is located superior to the lambdoidal suture in the occipital Yang region and is treated for the same range of neurological and neuropsychiatric conditions as its Yin counterpart. Diagnostic zones for all Brain Points are found on the abdomen and neck.
points.location: Located in the occipital Yang region, superior to the lambdoidal suture, approximately 1 cm lateral to the midline on both sides. The arrangement mirrors the Yin Brain Points.
points.reactionArea: The cerebrum and its associated cortical functions.

points.name: Cerebellum Point Yin
points.shortDescription: The Cerebellum Point is one of three YNSA Brain Points used to treat neurological and neuropsychiatric conditions. It has corresponding diagnostic zones on the abdomen and neck.
points.longDescription: The Cerebellum Point Yin lies approximately 1 cm lateral to the midline on both sides, in the same posterior line from Basic Point A8 as the Cerebrum Point. Together with the Cerebrum and Basal Ganglia Points, it forms the YNSA Brain Point group. The points originate just posterior to the edge of the galea aponeurotica, approximately 3 cm anterior to the coronal suture, extending 1.5–2 cm toward Basic Point A8.
points.location: Approximately 1 cm lateral to the midline on both sides, along a posteriorly directed line from Basic Point A8 — positioned more posteriorly along this line relative to the Cerebrum Point. The group begins just posterior to the galea aponeurotica, about 3 cm anterior to the coronal suture.
points.reactionArea: The cerebellum and its associated functions of balance and motor coordination.

points.name: Basal Ganglia Point Yin
points.shortDescription: The Basal Ganglia Point is one of three YNSA Brain Points used to treat neurological and neuropsychiatric conditions. It has an elongated shape and corresponding diagnostic zones on the abdomen and neck.
points.longDescription: The Basal Ganglia Point Yin is situated on the midline, between the Cerebrum and Cerebellum Points. Unlike the other two Brain Points, it has an elongated shape and extends slightly further posteriorly. Together the three Brain Points begin just posterior to the galea aponeurotica, approximately 3 cm anterior to the coronal suture, extending 1.5–2 cm toward Basic Point A8.
points.location: Located on the midline, between the Cerebrum Point and Cerebellum Point Yin. The point is elongated and extends slightly more posteriorly than the flanking brain points. The Brain Point group begins just posterior to the galea aponeurotica, about 3 cm anterior to the coronal suture.
points.reactionArea: The basal ganglia and their role in motor control and movement regulation.

points.name: Basal Ganglia Point Yang
points.shortDescription: The Basal Ganglia Point Yang is the occipital counterpart, sharing the same elongated morphology and neurological indications as the Yin point.
points.longDescription: The Basal Ganglia Point Yang is located in the occipital Yang region, superior to the lambdoidal suture, on the midline between the Cerebrum and Cerebellum Yang Points. It retains the elongated shape characteristic of the Basal Ganglia point and extends slightly more posteriorly than the flanking points. Diagnostic zones for all Brain Points are found on the abdomen and neck.
points.location: Located on the midline in the occipital Yang region, superior to the lambdoidal suture, between the Cerebrum Point Yang and Cerebellum Point Yang. The point is elongated and positioned slightly more posteriorly than the flanking brain points.
points.reactionArea: The basal ganglia and their role in motor control and movement regulation.

points.name: Cerebellum Point Yang
points.shortDescription: The Cerebellum Point Yang is the occipital counterpart to the Yin Cerebellum Point, sharing the same neurological indications.
points.longDescription: The Cerebellum Point Yang is located in the occipital Yang region, superior to the lambdoidal suture, and mirrors the Yin point in both arrangement and clinical use. It is treated for the full range of neurological conditions addressed by the YNSA Brain Point group. Diagnostic zones for all Brain Points are located on the abdomen and neck.
points.location: Located in the occipital Yang region, superior to the lambdoidal suture, approximately 1 cm lateral to the midline. Mirrors the arrangement of the Yin Brain Points.
points.reactionArea: The cerebellum and its associated functions of balance and motor coordination.

points.name: Mouth Point
points.shortDescription: The Mouth sensory point treats disorders affecting the mouth and oral cavity.
points.longDescription: The YNSA Sensory Points (Eye, Nose, Mouth, Ear) are arranged vertically in the frontal yin zone at approximately 1 cm intervals, starting from the lowest Basic A point and situated 1 cm lateral to the midline. A corresponding Yang representation of all sensory points is located in the occipital region.
points.location: Arranged vertically with the other sensory points (Eye, Nose) at approximately 1 cm intervals, starting from the lowest Basic A point, situated 1 cm lateral to the midline.
points.reactionArea: The mouth and oral cavity.
